{{short description|Field of research}}
{{about|effect of information technology on psychology|effect of psychology on cybersecurity|psychology in cybersecurity}}
{{Multiple issues|{{Lead too short|date=April 2023}}
{{Original research|date=April 2023}}
{{POV|reason=This article seems to focus on potential negative aspects of social media use rather than positive aspects, and often does so in a way that is not supported by reliable sources.|date=April 2023}}
}}

'''Cyberpsychology''' (also known as '''Internet psychology''', '''web psychology''', or '''digital psychology''') is a scientific [[Interdisciplinarity|inter-disciplinary]] domain that focuses on the [[Psychology|psychological]] phenomena which emerge as a result of the [[Interpersonal relationship|human interaction]] with digital [[technology]], particularly the [[Internet]].'''<ref name="Cyberpsychology Section">{{Cite web |title=Cyberpsychology Section |url=https://www.bps.org.uk/member-networks/cyberpsychology-section |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=BPS |language=en}}</ref>''' Cyberpsychology is a field that explores the psychological phenomena associated with cyberspace which includes study of human behaviour, emotions in online environments like social media, virtual reality and many more gaming platforms. Cyberpsychology also interacts with the areas like neuroscience, sociology and media studies that offers insights into how digital tool shape individual and collective experiences.{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

==Overview==
Cyberpsychology is a broadly used term for inter-disciplinary research that commonly describes how humans interact with others over technology, how human behavior and psychological states are affected by technology, and how technology can be optimally developed for human needs.<ref>{{Cite book |title=An Introduction to Cyberpsychology |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-82379-2 |editor-last=Connolly |editor-first=Irene |location=London New York |editor-last2=Palmer |editor-first2=Marion |editor-last3=Barton |editor-first3=Hannah |editor-last4=Kirwan |editor-first4=Gráinne}}</ref> While not explicitly defined as cyberpsychology, previous research into the impacts of [[virtual reality]] on human behavior<ref>{{cite book|last=Blascovich|first=Jim|title=Infinite reality: avatars, eternal life, new worlds, and the dawn of the virtual revolution|publisher=William Morrow|location=New York|isbn=978-0061809507|edition=1st|author2=Bailenson, Jeremy}}</ref> has been identified by cyberpsychologists and leveraged to guide the parameters of research areas. The importance of cyberpsychology as an independent and defined field from existing studies has been proposed by researchers such as Bruno Emond and Robert L West, suggesting the field should include cognitive modeling.<ref name=":13">{{Cite journal |last1=Emond |first1=Bruno |last2=West |first2=Robert L. |date=2003-10-01 |title=Cyberpsychology: A Human-Interaction Perspective Based on Cognitive Modeling |url=https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/109493103769710550 |journal=CyberPsychology & Behavior |volume=6 |issue=5 |pages=527–536 |doi=10.1089/109493103769710550 |pmid=14583128 |issn=1094-9313|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

While cyberpsychology remains broad, recent research has commonly been emerging on [[social media]]'s impact on [[personality disorder]]s, [[computer addiction]], [[video game addiction]], and online anxiety.<ref>{{Cite web |title=What is Cyberpsychology and Why is it Important? {{!}} New Jersey Institute of Technology |url=https://www.njit.edu/admissions/blog-posts/what-cyberpsychology-and-why-it-important |access-date=2024-02-04 |website=www.njit.edu}}</ref> The effects of [[Telehealth|virtual therapy]] have also been identified due to the global [[COVID-19 pandemic]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ancis |first=Julie R. |date=2020-09-18 |title=The Age of Cyberpsychology: An Overview |url=https://tmb.apaopen.org/pub/2yn6jhyv/release/1 |journal=Technology, Mind, and Behavior |language=en |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=9–23 |doi=10.1037/tmb0000009 |issn=2689-0208|doi-access=free }}</ref> These research areas also include the positive impact on the human psychological state regarding interaction with social [[artificial intelligence]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fortuna |first1=P |title=Positive cyberpsychology as a field of study of the well-being of people interacting with and via technology |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |date=2023 |volume=14|article-number=1053482 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1053482 |pmid=36910766 |pmc=9997032 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Research areas also include the influence of cyberpsychology on other fields; in research by Scott M. Debb, cyberpsychology is discussed as having interdependency with the discipline of [[Cybersecurity]], specifically regarding human subjects.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Debb |first=Scott M. |date=2021-09-01 |title=Keeping the Human in the Loop: Awareness and Recognition of Cybersecurity Within Cyberpsychology |url=https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2021.29225.sde |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |language=en |volume=24 |issue=9 |pages=581–583 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2021.29225.sde |pmid=34534015 |s2cid=237557065 |issn=2152-2715|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Professional bodies ==
The British Psychological Society<ref>{{Cite web |title=Homepage |url=https://www.bps.org.uk/node/2418 |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=BPS |language=en}}</ref> has a dedicated Cyberpsychology Section<ref name="Cyberpsychology Section"/> which was founded in 2018. Likewise the American Psychological Association has a dedicated division for Media Psychology & Technology.<ref>{{Cite web |title=APA homepage |url=https://www.apadivisions.org/division-46}}</ref> First published in 2007, Masaryk University in the Czech Republic has published ''Cyberpsychology: Journal of Psychosocial Research on Cyberspace'', an open access, web-based, peer-reviewed scholarly journal that focused on social science research about cyberspace.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Cyberpsychology: Journal of Psychosocial Research on Cyberspace |url=https://cyberpsychology.eu/ |access-date=2023-12-14 |website=cyberpsychology.eu |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Cyberpsychological behaviors==
It was around the turn of the millennium that the United States broke the 50 percent mark in Internet use, personal computer use, and cell phone use.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Norman |first=Kent L. |title=Cyberpsychology: An Introduction to Human-Computer Interaction |publisher=Cambridge University Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-1-107-10254-5 |location=United Kingdom |pages=9}}</ref> The relevance of [[human–computer interaction]] (HCI) research within the field of cyberpsychology may become more visible and necessary in understanding the current modern lifestyles of many people.

=== Social media ===
{{main|Fear of missing out}}
[[Facebook]], the leading online [[social media]] platform globally,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.statista.com/statistics/272014/global-social-networks-ranked-by-number-of-users/|title=Global social media ranking 2017 {{!}} Statistic|website=Statista|language=en|access-date=2017-12-05}}</ref> affects users' psychological status in multiple ways. Facebook follows the one-to-many communication pattern, allowing users to share information about their lives, including social activities and photographs.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Pempek|last2=Yermolayeva|last3=Calvert|journal=Journal of Applied Developmental Psychology|title=College students' social networking experiences on Facebook|volume=30:2|issue=2009|doi=10.1016/j.appdev.2008.12.010|pages=227–238|year=2009|s2cid=18165760 }}</ref> This feature was enhanced in 2012, when [[Facebook Messenger]] was implemented to allow users more one-on-one communication merging with the [[Facebook Messenger|Facebook Chat]] feature.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Social Media: A Reference Handbook|last=Burns|first=Kelli S.|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2017|isbn=978-1-4408-4355-6|location=Santa Barbara, CA|pages=18}}</ref> Facebook users enjoy the sense of being connected.<ref name="auto">{{cite journal|author1=Grieve|title=Face-to-Face or Facebook? Can social connectedness be derived online?|journal=Computers in Human Behavior|date=2013|pages=604–6099|display-authors=etal|doi=10.1016/j.chb.2012.11.017|volume=29|issue=3|s2cid=7232896 }}</ref> From a social standpoint, the internet is a breeding ground for creating a space for relationships, roles, and a new sense of self.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Emond |first1=Bruno |last2=West |first2=Robert L. |date=October 2003 |title=Cyberpsychology: A Human-Interaction Perspective Based on Cognitive Modeling |url=http://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/109493103769710550 |journal=CyberPsychology & Behavior |language=en |volume=6 |issue=5 |pages=527–536 |doi=10.1089/109493103769710550 |issn=1094-9313 |pmid=14583128|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Further, Facebook can be a tool in strengthening and reaffirming a relationship, as it allows for positive expressions of trust, affection, and commitment.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Stewart |first1=Margaret C. |last2=Dainton |first2=Marianne |last3=Goodboy |first3=Alan K. |date=2014-01-01 |title=Maintaining Relationships on Facebook: Associations with Uncertainty, Jealousy, and Satisfaction |journal=Communication Reports |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=13–26 |doi=10.1080/08934215.2013.845675 |issn=0893-4215 |s2cid=143973871}}</ref> People can derive a sense of social connectedness and belongingness in the online environment.<ref name="auto" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Grieve |first=Rachel |date=February 5, 2013 |title=Thumbs up: Facebook might actually be good for you |url=http://theconversation.com/thumbs-up-facebook-might-actually-be-good-for-you-11889 |website=The Conversation}}</ref> Messaging can also be used to express trust, affection, and commitment, thus strengthening personal relationships.<ref name=":1" /> This online social connectedness was associated with lower levels of depression and anxiety, and greater levels of subjective well-being.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Abebe |first1=Israel |last2=Wang |first2=Yu-Wei |last3=O’Connor |first3=Seini |last4=Cruz |first4=Tiana E. |last5=Keum |first5=Brian T. |last6=Sifat |first6=Munjireen |date=2023-07-31 |title=Satisfaction with online/in-person social interactions and psychological well-being: The mediating role of social connectedness |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-023-04870-0 |journal=Current Psychology |volume=43 |issue=10 |pages=8678 |doi=10.1007/s12144-023-04870-0 |issn=1046-1310|quote="The results showed that, after controlling for gender and  race, both satisfaction with social interactions through social media and with in-person interactions were significantly and  inversely related to self-reports of distress, anxiety, depression, anger, and loneliness. In addition, the findings from robust  mediation tests indicated that social connectedness mediated the path between satisfaction with social interactions and  the indices of well-being..."|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The size of an individual's online social network is closely linked to brain structure associated with social cognition.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kanai |first1=R. |last2=Bahrami |first2=B. |last3=Roylance |first3=R. |last4=Rees |first4=G. |date=2011-10-19 |title=Online social network size is reflected in human brain structure |journal=Proc. R. Soc. B |language=en |volume=279 |issue=1732 |pages=1327–1334 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2011.1959 |issn=0962-8452 |pmc=3282379 |pmid=22012980}}</ref> Because of the access people have had to internet technologies, some behaviors can be characterized as information foraging. Information foraging is the theory that when users have a certain information goal, they assess the information that they can extract from any candidate source of information relative to the cost involved in extracting that information and choose one or several candidate sources so that they maximize the ratio.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Budiu |first1=Raluca |title=EInformation Foraging: A Theory of How People Navigate on the Web |url=https://www.nngroup.com/articles/information-foraging/ |access-date=2 Feb 2023 |website=NNGroup}}</ref>

However, there are also downsides. A high level of Facebook usage is associated with adverse relationship outcomes (such as divorce and breaking up) and that these negative outcomes are mediated by conflict about high levels of Facebook use.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Clayton |last2=Nagurney |last3=Smith |date=2013 |title=Cheating, breakup, and divorce: Is Facebook use to blame?. |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |volume=16 |issue=10 |pages=717–720 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2012.0424 |pmid=23745615}}</ref> To cope with the uncertainty of a suspected romantic relationship, partner surveillance on Facebook is becoming more popular.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marshall |first1=Tara C. |last2=Bejanyan |first2=Kathrine |last3=Di Castro |first3=Gaia |last4=Lee |first4=Ruth A. |date=2013-03-01 |title=Attachment styles as predictors of Facebook-related jealousy and surveillance in romantic relationships |url=http://bura.brunel.ac.uk/handle/2438/6476 |journal=Personal Relationships |language=en |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=1–22 |doi=10.1111/j.1475-6811.2011.01393.x |issn=1475-6811 |s2cid=145421082}}</ref> However, this was only true for those who are or have been, in relatively newer relationships of three years or less. Excessive social media usage also increases feelings of [[social isolation]], as virtual relationships replace authentic social interactions.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite news |last=Molloy |first=Mark |date=2017-03-06 |title=Too much social media 'increases loneliness and envy' - study |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/2017/03/06/much-social-media-increases-loneliness-envy-study/ |access-date=2017-12-02 |work=The Telegraph |language=en-GB |issn=0307-1235}}</ref><ref name="childmind.org">{{Cite web |title=Does Social Media Use Cause Depression? |url=https://childmind.org/article/is-social-media-use-causing-depression/#social-media-and-depression |access-date=2025-05-08 |website=Child Mind Institute |language=en-us}}</ref>
Additionally, one study found that [[social rejection]] or [[ostracism]] in an [[Immersion (virtual reality)|immersive virtual environment]] has a negative impact on [[affect (psychology)|affect]] (emotion), in the same way, that ostracism negatively impacts emotions in real life contexts.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Matthew P. Kassner |author2=Eric D. Wesselmann |author3=Alvin Ty Law |author4=Kipling D. Williams |date=2012 |title=Virtually Ostracized: Studying Ostracism in Immersive Virtual Environments |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |volume=15 |issue=8 |pages=399–403 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2012.0113 |pmc=3422048 |pmid=22897472}}</ref> These findings do not demonstrate causality: relationship maintenance behaviors, such as surveillance and monitoring, are indicators of current levels of trust within the relationship.<ref name=":1" /> This suggests that certain behaviors on social media may be predicting negative outcomes, rather than causing them. When it comes to technology lot of people do not know when something has gone wrong until it goes wrong.<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Debb |first=Scott M. |date=2021-09-01 |title=Keeping the Human in the Loop: Awareness and Recognition of Cybersecurity Within Cyberpsychology |url=https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2021.29225.sde |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |volume=24 |issue=9 |pages=581–583 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2021.29225.sde |issn=2152-2715 |pmid=34534015 |s2cid=237557065|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Another byproduct of social media use is the "fear of missing out", or FOMO.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Rosen |first1=Larry Ph.D. |title=Always On, All the Time: Are We Suffering From FoMO? |url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/rewired-the-psychology-technology/201305/always-all-the-time-are-we-suffering-fomo |access-date=6 February 2015 |website=Psychology Today |publisher=Rewired: The Psychology of Technology}}</ref> This fear develops from a user's repetitive and obsessive status-checking of "friend" status updates and posts related to social events or celebrations resulting in a feeling of being "left out" if these events are not experienced.  There is also the closely related fear of being missed (FOBM), or the fear of invisibility.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Davis |first1=Jenny |title=From 'hyper' to 'in': on visibility |url=http://thesocietypages.org/cyborgology/2012/12/05/from-hyper-to-in-on-visibility/ |access-date=6 February 2015 |website=thesocietypages.org |publisher=Cyborgology}}</ref> This fear involves an obsessive need to provide constant status updates on one's own personal, day-to-day life, movements, travel, events, etc. unable to "un-plug". Evidence suggests this type of anxiety is a mediating factor in increased social media use and decreased self-esteem.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Buglass, Sarah L. |author2=Binder, Jens F. |author3=Betts, Lucy R. |author4=Underwood, Jean D.M. |date=January 2017 |title=Motivators of Online Vulnerability: The Impact of Social Network Site Use and FOMO |url=https://search.lib.virginia.edu/articles/article?id=edselp:S0747563216306902 |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |volume=66 |pages=248–255 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2016.09.055 |s2cid=10887386}}</ref> Users see only the joyous or entertaining experiences in a friend's life and compares them to their own lesser experiences.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Alexander H. Jordan, Benoît Monin, Carol S. Dweck, Benjamin J. Lovett, Oliver P. John, and James J. Gross |year=2011 |title=Misery Has More Company Than People Think: Underestimating the Prevalence of Others' Negative Emotions |url=http://www-psych.stanford.edu/~ajordan/papers/Jordan%20et%20al.%20(2011)%20-%20Misery%20Has%20More%20Company.pdf |url-status=dead |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=120–135 |doi=10.1177/0146167210390822 |pmc=4138214 |pmid=21177878 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151008220618/http://www-psych.stanford.edu/~ajordan/papers/Jordan%20et%20al.%20%282011%29%20-%20Misery%20Has%20More%20Company.pdf |archive-date=2015-10-08}}</ref>  Underestimating peers negative experiences correlates with greater loneliness and lower overall [[life satisfaction]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jordan |first1=Alexander H. |last2=Monin |first2=Benoît |last3=Dweck |first3=Carol S. |last4=Lovett |first4=Benjamin J. |last5=John |first5=Oliver P. |last6=Gross |first6=James J. |year=2010 |title=Misery Has More Company Than People Think: Underestimating the Prevalence of Others' Negative Emotions |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |language=en |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=120–135 |doi=10.1177/0146167210390822 |pmc=4138214 |pmid=21177878}}</ref> Inviting constant comparisons inevitably lowers self-esteem and feelings of self-worth; hence, Facebook and other social media accounts appear to exploit a vulnerability in human nature.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Copeland |first1=Libby |date=26 January 2011 |title=The Anti-Social Network |url=http://www.slate.com/articles/double_x/doublex/2011/01/the_antisocial_network.html |access-date=8 February 2015 |website=Slate.com}}</ref>

Short videos and memes are another aspect often seen in social media. Platforms like [[TikTok]] have been shown to negatively affect young college-aged adults with mental disorders as a result of their frequent usage as shown by one Chinese study which found negative cognition tendencies resulting in negative emotions.<ref name="journalarticle2">{{Cite journal |last1=Linlin |first1=Wang |last2=Wanyu |first2=Huang |date=2023-04-16 |title=Research on the mechanism of short video information interaction behavior of college students with psychological disorders based on grounded theory |journal=BMC Public Health |volume=23 |issue=1 |page=2256 |doi=10.1186/s12889-023-17211-4 |issn= |pmc=10652505 |pmid=37974096 |doi-access=free}}</ref> There is a murder inspired from a movie Halloween which was later uploaded in TikTok by a murderer.<ref>{{cite web | title=The Shocking Case of Claire Miller, the Teenage TikTok Star Who Brutally Murdered Her Disabled Sister | date=29 July 2023 | url=https://allthatsinteresting.com/claire-miller }}</ref> While getting arrested, she also mentioned, How she Michael Myers her big sister.<ref>{{cite web | title=When She Murdered Her Sister for TikTok Fame &#124; True Crime &#124; Wronged | website=[[YouTube]] | date=6 January 2024 | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tj6k6X4e70U }}</ref> Other researchers have also explored the manifestation of factitious disorder and dissociative identity disorder among recurrent users of social media platforms like TikTok.<ref name="journalarticle">{{Cite journal |last1=Giedinghagen |first1=Andrea |date=2022-04-27 |title=The tic in TikTok and (where) all systems go: Mass social media induced illness and Munchausen's by internet as explanatory models for social media associated abnormal illness behavior |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/13591045221098522 |journal=Clinical Child Psychology and Psychiatry |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=270–278 |doi=10.1177/13591045221098522 |issn= |pmid=35473358 |s2cid=248403566|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Florez |first=Joey |title=A Short Introduction to Psychology |publisher= |year=2023 |isbn=9798218173616 |edition= |location=New York |pages=9}}</ref> Memes, created images meant to inspire humor, have been shown to help people to interact successfully with other people online and to build a shared positive experience and allowing a glimpse into the formation of culture and language.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |author=Shifman, Limor |title=Memes in digital culture |year=2014 |isbn=978-0262525435 |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts |oclc=860711989}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Julien |first=Chris |year=2014 |title=Bourdieu, Social Capital and Online Interaction |url=https://zenodo.org/record/894539 |journal=Sociology |language=en |volume=49 |issue=2 |pages=356–373 |doi=10.1177/0038038514535862 |s2cid=144559268}}</ref>

=== Video games ===
Online gaming is a form of social activity that has been positively correlated with improving depressive symptoms in young individuals who otherwise lack a supportive social group.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kim |first1=Harris Hyun-soo |last2=Ahn |first2=Sun Joo Grace |date=October 2016 |title=How Does Neighborhood Quality Moderate the Association Between Online Video Game Play and Depression? A Population-Level Analysis of Korean Students |url=http://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2016.0155 |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |language=en |volume=19 |issue=10 |pages=628–634 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2016.0155 |pmid=27732076 |issn=2152-2715|url-access=subscription }}</ref> For adults, video games have been shown to improve cognitive abilities as well as reducing several mental illness symptoms.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite journal |last1=James |first1=Ella L. |last2=Bonsall |first2=Michael B. |last3=Hoppitt |first3=Laura |last4=Tunbridge |first4=Elizabeth M. |last5=Geddes |first5=John R. |last6=Milton |first6=Amy L. |last7=Holmes |first7=Emily A. |date=August 2015 |title=Computer Game Play Reduces Intrusive Memories of Experimental Trauma via Reconsolidation-Update Mechanisms |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=26 |issue=8 |pages=1201–1215 |doi=10.1177/0956797615583071 |issn=0956-7976 |pmc=4526368 |pmid=26133572}}</ref>

There has been controversy on the subject of violence in video games. Some studies have found that violent video games can lead to negative behavioral outcomes such as decreased empathy and an increase of aggressive behavior.<ref name="doi.apa.org">{{Cite journal |last1=Anderson |first1=Craig A. |last2=Shibuya |first2=Akiko |last3=Ihori |first3=Nobuko |last4=Swing |first4=Edward L. |last5=Bushman |first5=Brad J. |last6=Sakamoto |first6=Akira |last7=Rothstein |first7=Hannah R. |last8=Saleem |first8=Muniba |date=2010 |title=Violent video game effects on aggression, empathy, and prosocial behavior in Eastern and Western countries: A meta-analytic review. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0018251 |journal=Psychological Bulletin |language=en |volume=136 |issue=2 |pages=151–173 |doi=10.1037/a0018251 |pmid=20192553 |issn=1939-1455|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Video games being a primary source of entertainment, there are a few incidents inspired by video games that have led to unimaginable crimes in real life.<ref>{{cite web | title=15 Real Life Crimes Inspired by Video Games | date=14 September 2017 | url=https://www.thegamer.com/15-real-life-crimes-inspired-by-video-games/ }}</ref> Others have found little to no correlation with these behaviors being linked to violent video games.<ref name="Psychosocial Effects of Gaming">{{Citation |last1=Carras |first1=Michelle Colder |title=Psychosocial Effects of Gaming |date=2019-05-16 |work=The Oxford Handbook of Cyberpsychology |pages=556–587 |editor-last=Attrill-Smith |editor-first=Alison |url=https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/41352/chapter/352516594 |access-date=2025-04-18 |publisher=Oxford University Press |language=en |doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198812746.013.30 |isbn=978-0-19-881274-6 |last2=Kowert |first2=Rachel |last3=Quandt |first3=Thorsten |editor2-last=Fullwood |editor2-first=Chris |editor3-last=Keep |editor3-first=Melanie |editor4-last=Kuss |editor4-first=Daria J.|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Overall, despite over 30 years of research, there is no definitive evidence of a connection with these behaviors and video games.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Calvert |first1=Sandra L. |last2=Appelbaum |first2=Mark |last3=Dodge |first3=Kenneth A. |last4=Graham |first4=Sandra |last5=Nagayama Hall |first5=Gordon C. |last6=Hamby |first6=Sherry |last7=Fasig-Caldwell |first7=Lauren G. |last8=Citkowicz |first8=Martyna |last9=Galloway |first9=Daniel P. |last10=Hedges |first10=Larry V. |date=February 2017 |title=The American Psychological Association Task Force assessment of violent video games: Science in the service of public interest. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0040413 |journal=American Psychologist |language=en |volume=72 |issue=2 |pages=126–143 |doi=10.1037/a0040413 |pmid=28221065 |issn=1935-990X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Addiction has also been tied to gaming and is an area that is considered to be valuable for future study.<ref name="doi.org">{{Cite book |url=https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.books.9780890425596.893619 |title=Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition |date=2013 |publisher=American Psychiatric Publishing, Inc |doi=10.1176/appi.books.9780890425596.893619 |isbn=978-0-89042-559-6}}</ref> The World Health Organization currently recognizes Gaming Disorder as a mental health condition.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Oltmanns |first=Joshua R. |date=2020-12-16 |title=Personality Traits in the International Classification of Diseases 11th Revision (ICD-11) |doi=10.31234/osf.io/wyu47 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Conditions of this disorder largely refer to an increasing lack of interest in other activities and signs of addictive behavior leading to more game usage.<ref name=":4" /> However, there are those who believe that there is a difference between those that show these conditions but experience a positive impact and those that experience more negative outcomes and further research is needing to be done in order to definitely prove the positive or negative outcomes that gaming can lead to.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kuss |first1=Daria J. |last2=Griffiths |first2=Mark D. |last3=Pontes |first3=Halley M. |date=2017-06-20 |title=DSM-5 diagnosis of Internet Gaming Disorder: Some ways forward in overcoming issues and concerns in the gaming studies field: Response to the commentaries |journal=Journal of Behavioral Addictions |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=133–141 |doi=10.1556/2006.6.2017.032 |issn=2062-5871 |pmc=5520128 |pmid=28662619}}</ref> For instance, some studies have suggested that gaming is a result of negative health conditions, not the root cause of them.<ref name="Psychosocial Effects of Gaming"/>

===Psychotherapy in cyberspace===
Psychotherapy in cyberspace is also known as cybertherapy or e-therapy.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ciccarelli |first1=Saundra K. |url=https://archive.org/details/psychology03edcicc |title=Psychology |last2=White |first2=J. Noland |date=2010-12-31 |publisher=Pearson Prentice Hall |isbn=9780205832576 |url-access=registration}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=January 2009 |title=Ethical implications of verbal disinhibition with CAs |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/220168786 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250714043530/https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Antonella-De-Angeli/publication/220168786_Ethical_implications_of_verbal_disinhibition_with_CAs/links/54a6a5f80cf267bdb909db3e/Ethical-implications-of-verbal-disinhibition-with-CAs.pdf?origin=journalDetail&_sg%5B0%5D=started_experiment_milestone |archive-date=2025-07-14 |access-date=2025-09-05 |website=www.researchgate.net}}</ref> The first instance of this practice did not include interaction with a human, but rather a program called [[ELIZA]], which was designed by [[Joseph Weizenbaum]] to answer questions and concerns with basic Rogerian responses.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Duggan |first=Geoffrey B. |date=2016-07-02 |title=Applying psychology to understand relationships with technology: from ELIZA to interactive healthcare |journal=Behaviour & Information Technology |volume=35 |issue=7 |pages=536–547 |doi=10.1080/0144929X.2016.1141320 |issn=0144-929X |s2cid=30885893}}</ref>  ELIZA proved to be so convincing that many people either mistook the program for human, or became emotionally attached to it.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Suler |first=John R. |author-link=John Suler |date=2000-04-01 |title=Psychotherapy in Cyberspace: A 5-Dimensional Model of Online and Computer-Mediated Psychotherapy |journal=CyberPsychology & Behavior |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=151–159 |doi=10.1089/109493100315996 |issn=1094-9313}}</ref>

In [[online counseling]], a person e-mails or chats online with a therapist. There are also new applications of technology within psychology and healthcare which utilize augmented and virtual reality components—for example in pain management treatment, PTSD treatment, use of avatars in virtual environments, and self- and clinician-guided computerized cognitive behavior therapies.<ref>See [http://www.fenichel.com/psychtech2011.shtml Technology and Psychology 2011]</ref> The voluminous work of Azy Barak<ref>Barak, A., & Suler, J. (2008). [https://web.archive.org/web/20130930185310/http://construct.haifa.ac.il/~azy/01-Barak%26Suler.pdf Reflections on the psychology and social science of cyberspace]. In A. Barak & J. Suler (Eds.), ''Psychological aspects of cyberspace: Theory, Research, Applications'' (pp. 1–12). Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|9780521873017}}</ref> ([[University of Haifa]]) and a growing number of researchers in the US and UK gives strong evidence to the efficacy (and sometimes superiority) of Internet-facilitated, computer-assisted treatments relative to 'traditional' in-office-only approaches. The UK's National Health Service now recognizes CCBT (computerized [[cognitive behavioral therapy]]) as the preferred method of treatment for mild-to-moderate presentations of anxiety and depression.<ref>See [http://www.fenichel.com/CCBT.shtml August 2011 presentation by Kate Cavanaugh, author of "Hands on Help"]</ref>

== Impacts on mental health ==

=== ADHD ===
An emerging body of research suggests that internet addiction and excessive social media usage or gaming activity may be more prevalent in ADHD individuals due to people diagnosed with ADHD being more likely to struggling with organizing or managing time.<ref name=":2">{{cite web |last1=Crowell |first1=Grant |title=Social Media and ADHD: Turning Distractions into Directions |url=http://www.add.org/?page=Social_Media_n_ADHD |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190427001149/https://add.org/?page=Social_Media_n_ADHD |archive-date=27 April 2019 |access-date=8 February 2015 |website=ADD.org}}</ref> Male college students are more likely than women college students to be screened positively for adult ADHD; however, the overall association between Internet addiction and attention deficit is more significant in females.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Yen |first1=Ju-Yu |date=2009 |title=The Association Between Adult ADHD Symptoms and Internet Addiction Among College Students |journal=CyberPsychology & Behavior |volume=12 |issue=2 |pages=187–191 |doi=10.1089/cpb.2008.0113 |pmid=19072077}}</ref> In the view of Dr. Robert Melillo, founder of the [[Brain Balance|<u>Brain Balance Program</u>]], "When kids play computer games, their minds are processing information in a much different way than kids who are, say, running around on a playground... Recent studies have shown that playing computer games only builds very short-term attention that needs to be rewarded frequently."<ref>{{Cite book |last=Melillo |first=Dr. Robert |title=Disconnected Kids |publisher=Perigee |year=2015 |isbn=978-0-399-53475-1 |location=United States |pages=60–80}}</ref>

=== Mental illness ===
{{see also|Internet addiction disorder}}
Studies have shown that social media, and Facebook in particular, can be a factor in depression, especially among teenage users.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Facebook Calls Links To Depression Inconclusive. These Researchers Disagree |language=en |work=NPR.org |url=https://www.npr.org/2021/05/18/990234501/facebook-calls-links-to-depression-inconclusive-these-researchers-disagree |access-date=2022-10-12}}</ref> Social psychologist [[Ethan Kross]] led a study that investigated how a person's mood fluctuated during time spent on Facebook and whether or not they modified their Facebook usage.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.universityherald.com/articles/4230/20130816/facebook-use-bad-self-esteem-matter-why-log.htm|title=Facebook Use Bad For Self-Esteem No Matter Why You Log On|last1=Westerholm|first1=Russell|website=University Herald|date=16 August 2013|access-date=10 June 2014}}</ref> Results suggest that as participants spent more time on Facebook, their feelings of well-being decreased and feelings of depression increased.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://guardianlv.com/2013/08/facebook-causes-depression-new-study-says/#PzRdcP38KPwjLoxo.9|title=Facebook Cause Depression New Study Says|last1=Savastio|first1=Rebecca|website=Liberty Voie|date=15 August 2013|access-date=10 June 2014}}</ref> Another study found that participants in the highest quartile for social media site visits per week were at an increased likelihood of experiencing depression.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Liu yi Lin, Jaime E. Sidani, Ariel Shensa, Ana Radovic, Elizabeth Miller|display-authors=et al|date=2016|title=Association Between Social Media Use and Depression Among U.S. Young Adults|journal=Depression and Anxiety|volume=33|issue=4|pages=323–331|via=PsycINFO|doi=10.1002/da.22466|pmid=26783723|pmc=4853817}}</ref>

Addiction has also been tied to online usage. This may be because people are learning to access and process information more rapidly and to shift attention quickly from one task to the next. All this access and vast selection is causing some entertainment seekers to develop the constant need for instant gratification with a loss of patience.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Jaclyn Cabral |date=2011 |title=Is Generation Y Addicted to Social Media? |url=http://www.elon.edu/docs/e-web/academics/communications/research/vol2no1/01Cabral.pdf |url-status=dead |journal=The Elon Journal of Undergraduate Research in Communications |volume=2 |issue=1 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201114004024/http://www.elon.edu/docs/e-web/academics/communications/research/vol2no1/01Cabral.pdf |archive-date=2020-11-14 |access-date=2015-02-08}}</ref> Results from a survey of university undergraduates showed that almost 10% met criteria for what investigators describe as "disordered social networking use".<ref name="auto1">{{cite web |last1=Hackethal |first1=Veronica MD. |date=December 16, 2014 |title=Social Media Potentially Addictive, Linked to Substance Abuse |url=http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/836660 |access-date=7 February 2015 |website=Medscape}}</ref> Respondents who met criteria for "Facebook addiction" also reported statistically significant symptoms similar to the symptoms of addiction, such as tolerance (increased Facebook use over time), withdrawal (irritability when unable to access Facebook), and cravings to access the site. "Our findings suggest that there may be shared mechanisms underlying both substance and behavioral addictions," Hormes added.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Julia M. Hormes |author2=Brianna Kearns |author3=C. Alix Timko |year=2014 |title=Craving Facebook? Behavioral addiction to online social networking and its association with emotion regulation deficits |journal=Addiction |volume=109 |issue=12 |pages=2079–2088 |doi=10.1111/add.12713 |pmid=25170590}}</ref> The prevalence of internet addiction varies considerably between countries and is inversely related to the quality of life.<ref>Cheng Cecilia and Li Angel Yee-lam. Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking. December 2014, 17(12): 755–760. http://dx.doi:10{{Dead link|date=July 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}.1089/cyber.2014.0317.</ref> Many countries in Asia (particularly China, South Korea, and Japan) have raised public concern over the recent rise in internet addictions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Block |first=Jerald J. |date=March 2008 |title=Issues for DSM-V: Internet Addiction |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.2007.07101556 |journal=American Journal of Psychiatry |volume=165 |issue=3 |pages=306–307 |doi=10.1176/appi.ajp.2007.07101556 |issn=0002-953X |pmid=18316427|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Behavioral impacts ===
Social media at use can lead to lower quality sleep.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Garett |last2=Liu |last3=Young |date=2018 |title=The Relationship Between Social Media Use and Sleep Quality among Undergraduate Students |journal=Information, Communication & Society |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=163–173 |doi=10.1080/1369118X.2016.1266374 |pmc=5881928 |pmid=29628784}}</ref> A study commissioned by Travelodge hotels<ref name=":5">{{cite web |date=6 December 2010 |title=Bedtime Social Networking Causes Sleep Deprivation Epidemic Across Britain |url=http://www.travelodge.co.uk/press_releases/press_release.php?id=408 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111126143839/http://www.travelodge.co.uk/press_releases/press_release.php?id=408 |archive-date=26 November 2011}}</ref> concluded that Britain has become a nation of 'Online-A-Holics'. The study found Britons spend 16 minutes in bed socially networking with pals each night – the peak chatting time being 9:45 pm and are getting just six hours and 21 minutes of sleep per night.<ref name=":5" />

Other studies have found a correlation between social media use and [[eating disorders|disordered eating]].<ref>{{cite web |author=Elish, Jill |date=March 6, 2014 |title=Hungry for 'likes': Facebook use linked to eating disorder risk |url=http://fsu.edu/indexTOFStory.html?lead.facebook_study |access-date=March 23, 2016 |publisher=Florida State University}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Rojas |first1=Marcela |date=June 1, 2014 |title=Social Media Helps Fuel Some Eating Disorders |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/nation/2014/06/01/social-media-helps-fuel-eating-disorders/9817513/ |access-date=6 February 2015 |website=USA Today |publisher=The (Westchester County, N.Y.) Journal News}}</ref><ref name="Harrison 2006">{{cite journal |author1=K Harrison |author2=J Cantor |date=February 7, 2006 |title=The Relationship Between Media Consumption and Eating Disorders |journal=Journal of Communication |volume=47 |issue=1 |pages=40–67 |doi=10.1111/j.1460-2466.1997.tb02692.x}}</ref> In women college students, social media use predicts disordered-eating symptomatology and other related variables (such as the drive for thinness and body dissatisfaction).<ref name="Harrison 2006" /> For men, media use predicted endorsement of personal thinness and dieting.<ref name="Harrison 2006" />

==In popular culture==
{{in popular culture|section|date=April 2026}}
* [[Lisa Kudrow]]'s Web-based situation comedy ''[[Web Therapy]]'', in which Kudrow's unaccredited and unscrupulous character Fiona Wallice conducts therapy sessions using iChat, explores many of the ethical and practical issues raised by the prospect of psychotherapy conducted via Internet video chat.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://plasmapool.org/2010/12/07/web-therapy/|title=Web Therapy - Plasma Pool|work=plasmapool.org|access-date=2011-10-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111010183324/http://plasmapool.org/2010/12/07/web-therapy/|archive-date=2011-10-10|url-status=dead}}</ref>
* [[Patricia Arquette]] recurs as FBI Special Agent in Charge [[Avery Ryan]], a cyberpsychologist, in ''[[CSI: Crime Scene Investigation]]''. She also headlines the spinoff series ''[[CSI: Cyber]]'' in the same role.<ref>{{Citation|title=CSI: Crime Scene Investigation|url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0247082/|others=Laurence Fishburne, Marg Helgenberger, George Eads|access-date=2017-12-06}}</ref>
* [[Forensic anthropology|Forensic anthropologist]] Dr. Temperance Brennan and Special Agent Seeley Booth in Fox Network's hit television series, ''[[Bones (TV series)|Bones]]'', practice cyberpsychology by collecting information from suspects' social media accounts to analyze personality, communications, and possible motives to help apprehend the criminal.<ref>{{Citation|title=Bones|url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0460627/|others=Emily Deschanel, David Boreanaz, Michaela Conlin|access-date=2017-12-06}}</ref>
